item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a bit late but if I could give a bit (edit: scratch that, this got way too long, I apologize in advance) of criticism, based on the descriptions I have a very very hard time visualizing the scale and movement of whatever-it-is. This is really important to help me mentally understand the threat it presents and why it's frightening. It can eat entire islands, but can also reach down [something] (its head? does it have multiple heads?) and bite two specific individuals in half. It can also reach high enough to get a helicopter. But if it's so big it eats whole islands, it's not going to bother with a helicopter, is it? That's like swatting a gnat.

If it's the size of a T-Rex then I can see why it would bother to reach down and eat two individuals and might even be able to bite them in half. But a T-Rex or even something several times larger can't reach a helicopter and certainly doesn't have the scale to wipe an island or town off the map. If it's large enough to somehow consume an entire island, it's not going to bother reaching down to eat individual people because it's somehow consuming the land itself.

+ show block
– hide block

The way the reporter describes it also feels strange to me. She's describing its overwhelmingly massive size but also with the "fetid" talk it's as if she can see it in great detail like it's close to her. I don't really know how to put this but it feels like those two things are at odds. If it's that big and that close as she's describing it then the way she's talking just doesn't go together in my mind. And it sounds like in the blink of an eye she goes from being on a building's fire escape (so multiple floors off the ground) to seemingly running in the streets. Again, I'm not understanding the physical positions and distances involved, which is really important to visualizing what's going on so I know what I should be frightened of.

Leaving things to the reader's imagination can obviously be really good since they're going to imagine something personally frightening to them, but I can't even form the basis of a mental image when there's so much conflicting information. Or maybe I'm super dumb/tired right now and I missed something critical.

The some-records-continue-to-exist-some-don't thing also doesn't work for me. It doesn't seem like there's an underlying pattern, like "messages sent or data stored under these specific conditions persists while everything else disappears"… it feels like there's a chill waiting to go down my spine as I as the reader realize, oh my god, this is the thing tying these events together and it's dangerous and the Foundation doesn't realize it…! But I got to the end and didn't get anything. A website that sorta exists but doesn't, a photo that doesn't really show much of anything, some random assorted text messages, a map with a road to nowhere. I want to find out that the places that were attacked were all locations where PROPOSED Foundation sites were going to be built in the future, like there's something from another universe instinctively attacking its masters that don't exist here, or… I don't know. Maybe the locations that were attacked all had something else in common that the article doesn't explicitly state but the viewer can read between the lines and figure it out for themselves. Maybe that's already there but I'm too dumb/tired, but I just skimmed it again and didn't see anything.

Maybe the algae's a clue to its nature and I'm just too tired to do the mental math.

Sorry if I'm rambling or not making sense, really tired. Basically just saying I think the vagueness weakens the story rather than strengthens it, the descriptions have inconsistencies that detract rather than add.

I LOVE the concept of this incomprehensible gigantic being that nobody's aware of, that's wiping whole cities or islands off the map maybe at this very moment, but it projects a somebody-else's-problem field that means we could potentially watch it happening from a safe distance, millions of people dying, all this destruction, and we don't mentally notice what's going on even as we're staring at it. And when it's done, nobody knows those places ever existed, except for specific criteria that it somehow fails to wipe out. There's this nemesis of humanity out there and maybe in 50 years we'll be down to our last city and not even realize we had a greater civilization at one point.

But what's that criteria? What's this thing's agenda? Where did it come from? How big is it? How fast does it move? What's with this algae it leaves behind? I don't need to be spoon-fed but I need a little more than you're giving me.

Oh also I noticed at least one "it's" where there should be an "its": [It moves slow, like it’s enormous weight is]. Might be others.


As a courtesy to our readers on mobile devices, please collapse long posts. ~Zyn

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The thing that weirds me out about this — and I can't tell if this is coincidence or it's supposed to mean something — is that the kaiju-whatever-thing doesn't seem to have any problem moving between the Pacific and the Atlantic, but apparently sticks to the Northern hemisphere exclusively.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I… am still disheartened and confused that this didn't get more upvotes. It's… very strong.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice SCP. I like the idea of and anti-memetic monster ravaging throughout time and space. The start was iffy, but logs 1-3 and 6-7 are the meat of the SCP. I do feel like some compacting is necessary though, and either make the point of the logs about Dr. Kikuchi to have something recognized or just ditch it personally. Anyways, it was a good SCP.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is nice and eerie for what is ultimately an antimemetic kaiju, and the variety and specificity of the case studies really helps to hammer that home. This reads a lot closer to the 'classic' literature on Forteana or UFOlogy than most of the stuff on the site, thanks to the antimemetic elements of the anomaly, and I really appreciate it. I can see the argument that this runs a little long and has too many examples, but frankly I liked all of them. +1

EDIT: Also, 'March 17th 1998 was not a Sunday' immediately made me check if it was, in fact, a Sunday, because either answer is troubling but the answer fundamentally defines certain elements of the article. It's a nice stinger.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Suggesting 'give off low levels of radiation, and exhibit an isotope signature'; radiation itself only comes in four flavors, but the Foundation can trivially verify the presence of typical fallout elements/isotopes and their ratios.

If the stuff specifically doesn't have the expected elements present but is giving off the radiation, then I'd either be explicit about that or omit the mention of isotopes at all.

The Foundation also knows exactly what sort of radiation flux you read near the site of a nuclear detonation, so I'd find that perfectly compelling too.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting. Slime-mould godzilla that erases everything in the path of its rampage from history? Certainly a fresh take on the cliche.

Some editorial comments:

Initial account, which I might be looking at wrong?:

+ show block
– hide block
  • You can't get a GPS fix off a cell tower no matter how good the signal or how closely spaced they are. A phone is only ever connected to one at a time and doesn't triangulate; all you can ever get is the GPS coords of the tower a phone was connected to at the time of interest, and an estimated distance (more accurately, an estimated radius) from said tower based on signal strength and round-trip time… if the service provider bothers to calculate and store such information. From which you could take an educated guess as to a range of bearings from the tower to the phone, based on what other towers might be around, closer to the handset and with line of sight, which it would most likely have preferentially connected to instead, eliminating those directions from the most-likely list.
  • » Or in other words, all you can really say is that the phone was connected to that tower, and therefore most likely within its geographic "cell" (where the cellular network / cellphone name comes from) where that tower's signal is strongest. In busy areas said cells may be only a few hundred metres in size and you can actually use them to navigate with the aid of an internet-loaded map and a bit of orienteering and dead reckoning skill, but in rural areas they can easily cover a couple of kilometres radius and be no good for anything other than telling you you're on the right "page" of the map. Possibly if it regularly flits back and forth between two, or better yet three or four particular adjacent towers you could have a go at triangulating, or at least using that as an argument to limit your search to within a polygon drawn around those masts, but that's as good as it gets.
  • » » Actual GPS location requires, well, signals from the GPS satellites. If the phone doesn't have a fix from them (like my own hopeless handset usually doesn't), and/or doesn't embed that information into the message headers, you're shit out of luck. But that would be a fairly all-or-nothing thing in of itself; if you can get any kind of GPS fix at all, then it should be accurate to no worse than a few tens to a couple hundred metres, and if you've got one that's at all stable then the accuracy should be better than 10m…

others:

  • The US wasn't materially invested in the long running Vietnamese civil war in 1953; France was, as Vietnam (or, at the time, "Indochina") was its rebellious colony. America simply provided them, and the friendlier southern government, with aid - planes, weapons, etc, and didn't actually put boots on the ground until well into the 1960s. They couldn't initially afford to divert troops to directly help the fight, so soon after WW2, because what remained of their main fighting force was occupied in Korea instead, through to the ceasefire in late 1953, as documented by e.g. M*A*S*H. Given the appearance of a medevac helicopter, a familiar sight from that show's opening credits, could it be you were thinking of the Korean war instead? It would be a fairly quick fix if so. (Of course, if it - along with the Dustoff thing - is meant to be a hint towards the recording having fallen through a gap in spacetime from an alternate Erf where the US got involved in Vietnam a good ten years or more before it did here, then comment rescinded…)
  • Some typos and such at the end of the Russian account. "covered it feted" instead of "covered in fetid" (or foetid, if you prefer), and another missing word the final sentence. Seeing as it's supposed to be a transcript of an audio recording, and there's no [sic] or other signifier that the unusual language is original to the recording, I have to assume a mistype.
  • Can't really make out what that photo's supposed to show. It's a little bit weird and creepy, but the image itself doesn't really make any sense to me, other than having what might be a snake tail in it. Particularly, what's that artificial looking thing right in the middle?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For some reason when i saw the

"March 17th, 1998 was not a Sunday."

I nearly pissed myself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The writing could use work in a few places. The end of event 3 in particular has some rather bad sentences.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I will heavily consider that. Thank you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually liked the diversity portrayed by the various types of locations and the sweep of history here. While I agree that it was a satisfying story, the audio log was excellent and the verisimilitude lent for a pleasant read.

If I was suggesting logs to cut, perhaps I would cut the log from Wales and the one from Neva Bay, as they didn't really add anything. I would put the details from Event 5 into the log from Event 4, and put it last, swapping it with Event 7. That way you have a article with no fat that ends on a punch, which people tend to like.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I appreciate the comments (and the no vote, tbh.) I'm trying to think of an actionable way to look at yours and Shaggy's objection.

If I'm honest with myself, I could see cutting it down entirely to the twenty texts and the recording about the bridge. Maybe the Polaroid because it's a kind of different response. But of course, I don't want to do that because… I'm a writer. :D I do also want to show a span of time.

I'm going to give it some thought. A diversity of response sounds good, but I have to… come up with them. :D Thanks again to both of you all for the input.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Echoing shaggy's thoughts above.

Your concept is actually quite good, and there is a lot of narrative potential in a massive force of destruction that turns around and leaves no trace. That being said, your logs get very repetitive, very quickly. You basically start spinning your wheels past log 4.

Perhaps if there was more variety of outcome outside of "Holy shit, it's so big" it'd carry itself a little further. Right now, however, its an interesting premise that bogs itself down in the execution.

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for your thoughts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's got a very strong opening with a premise that combines several common tropes in a way that feels fresh, and the variety in the type of logs is a nice touch. That being said, I started to get tired from the fifth log on, as the story really doesn't continue to progress - instead presenting more or less the same scenario in various perspectives.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you so much. I'd tell you what you're missing, but I don't know where you sit on it so far. :D (If indeed you're missing anything at all.) I do feel like there's a second punch in it, but no, not everyone is catching it. Ah well.

I'm sure the Foundation would like to have some plan too. But. I think it's also interesting to look at a skip that they don't know enough about yet? Maybe in twenty years they'll know more. But. It's got to happen from time to time, maybe?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you so much! I fixed it. I appreciate you letting me know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

I had a ton of fun reading the event logs. But I feel theres some kind of fridge horror I'm not completely getting and if so it might need to be hinted at just a little better.

I'd also prefer to see the Foundation have at least some plan of how to contain this thing, but hey ho.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In Event 5 the first two paragraphs are the same.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really appreciate that! Look forward to your upvote later! Thanks for the comment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The description didn't catch me, but by the time I hit the discovery log as well as that first series of texts, you had me. The discovery in particular is very well written, not something I say often. Upvote after the contest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That’s what it is. Some times it’s about the journey not the destination. Sorry it didn’t do enough for you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So unless I'm missing something here, this is just a Kaiju that erases evidence of it's own existence. This isn't a terrible article by any stretch, but the concept is still just a Kaiju played almost completely straight.

Feel free to correct me if I'm missing something.
-1

Edit: reread and changed to no vote. Writing in the addenda was too good for me to downvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
682? When did you breach containment?

Nah really, this is good. I wouldn’t say it has the kind of splash that would make it the 4000 contest winner, but it’s a good skip in overall.

I like the idea of the Foundation investigating into things that have sort of already happened, when they’re left to find and make sense of the stuff that remains. It’s a solemn kind of aesthetic.

It gets an upvote from me. i’d like to see the whole thing explained in the future because it’s all mysterious-like

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All images used in this document are made by me using free material from https://www.pexels.com.

Radio announcer is voiced by Edward Bosco and used with his kind permission. http://www.edwardbosco.com

Thank you to the various discord users who have helped with formatting, editing, and commentary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ya'll are fast. Wow. Fixing that, and commenting here with credits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Withholding my vote until you fix the numerous instances of SCP-XXXX.
